macro_segments.title: Introduction to Kiddushin 58: Forbidden Objects in Benefit and Their Use in Marriage

micro_segments.title: Opening context: Objects forbidden in benefit and the question of whether they can be used for kiddushin
Chullin shenishchatu ba'azarah: non-sanctified animals slaughtered in the Temple courtyard
Learning the prohibition from the pasuk about distance from the Beis HaMikdash

macro_segments.title: Dispute of Rabbi Shimon vs. Chachamim on Chullin Shenishchatu Ba'azarah

micro_segments.title: Braita teaching Rabbi Shimon's position that petar chamor, basar bechalav, and chullin shenishchatu ba'azarah allow marriage
Logical basis for Rabbi Shimon's position on each of the three forbidden items
Gemara concludes: for Rabbi Shimon, chullin shenishchatu ba'azarah is lav de-oraisa (not biblically forbidden)

macro_segments.title: Understanding 'Lav De-Oraisa': Biblical vs. Rabbinic Prohibition and Object Status

micro_segments.title: Rashi's explanation: not the act but the status of the meat afterwards is not biblically forbidden
Question: Can objects rabanically forbidden in benefit be used for kiddushin despite rabbinic prohibition?
Tosafot on whether rabanically forbidden objects are considered valueless for kiddushin purposes

macro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Tam vs. Rashi: The Nature of Chametz and Stringency of Prohibited Objects

micro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Tam: wheat is real chametz but timing makes it rabbinic, giving it weight of biblical category
Rashi's alternative explanation of the chametz case in Pesachim
Tosafot's distinction: extension of biblical category vs. whole cloth rabbinic category

macro_segments.title: Challenge to Rabbi Shimon: Burning Chullin Shenishchatu Ba'azarah Despite Permissibility for Marriage

micro_segments.title: Mar Yehuda's challenge to Rav Yosef and Shmuel bar Rabba on the contradiction in Rabbi Shimon
Rava's response: contextualizing the case to when the animal was treifah
Shechita she'eina re'uya: slaughter that doesn't make meat permissible, creating exception to chullin ba'azarah status

macro_segments.title: Braita on Treifah Animal: Rabbi Shimon vs. Chachamim on Status of Invalid Slaughter

micro_segments.title: Teaching: slaughtering a known treifah or discovering animal is treifah after slaughter
Rabbi Shimon permits benefit because shechita she'eina re'uya; Chachamim forbid due to physical act

macro_segments.title: Transition: From Forbidden Objects to Benefit Transfers via Money and Exceptions

micro_segments.title: Principle: selling forbidden objects transfers status to proceeds, which can then be used for kiddushin
Gemara learns exception from idol: status transfers by avodah zarah and shemitah only
Two verses teach two cases therefore exceptions not rules; principle is status does not transfer

macro_segments.title: Shemitah Produce: Status Transfers Continuously Through Exchanges

micro_segments.title: Redemption analogy: shemitah status transfers to money and object exits status through pidyon
Successive exchanges: last object in swap retains shemitah status, original fruit always retains it
Comparison of avodah zarah and shemitah as exceptions where status transfers

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Using Terumot, Ma'asrot, Temple Gifts, and Chatas Water/Ash for Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: Mishnah lists: terumah, ma'aser, matanot (priest gifts), mei chatas, eifer parah—all enable marriage
Terumah and ma'aser are given to kohanim and leviim; question of value and ownership
Mei chatas and eifer parah used for purification; question of status and value for marriage

macro_segments.title: Ulla's Teaching: Tovas Hana'ah (Benefit of Choosing Recipient) Not Monetary Value

micro_segments.title: Ulla: right to decide which kohen receives terumah is benefit, not monetary ownership
Context interpretation: man separated terumah from grain obligated to be given to kohen
Woman gets benefit of choosing which kohen/levi to give the obligatory gift to

macro_segments.title: Rav Abba's Challenge: How Can Tovas Hana'ah Allow Marriage if It's Not Money?

micro_segments.title: Challenge: Mishnah teaches marriage works with terumah, contradicting that tovas hana'ah is not mamon
Easy answer: man owns the terumah itself (inherited from maternal grandfather or acquired)
Rashi: easy answer makes mishnah trivial, so harder case must be intended

macro_segments.title: Inherited Tevel from Kohen Grandfather: Ownership Through Potential Gifts

micro_segments.title: Hard case: man inherited tevel (unprocessed grain) from maternal grandfather who was a kohen
Teaching: matanot shelo hurmu k'mi shehurmu—gifts needed are considered already present in tevel
Therefore grandfather's kohen status gives ownership of terumah even before separation; man can keep it

macro_segments.title: Rav Chiyya bar Avin's Question to Rav Huna on Tovas Hana'ah as Monetary Value

micro_segments.title: Direct question: is tovas hana'ah (benefit of choosing) considered money or not?
Rav Huna's answer: look at our mishnah about marriage with terumah—that answers it
Chiyya's clarification: but you already explained it as tevel inherited from kohen grandfather
Rav's deflection: your explanation is excluded/not serious; I meant tevel case

macro_segments.title: Debate on Stealing Tevel: Does Owner Have Monetary Stake in Obligatory Gifts?

micro_segments.title: Case: stealing tevel from non-kohen—must thief pay full value or only for non-obligatory portion?
Rabbi: pay full value of tevel; Rabbi Yose bar Yehuda: pay only non-terumah portion
Possible interpretation: debate about whether tovas hana'ah (ability to give away) equals monetary ownership

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation: Debate About Tevel from Kohen Ancestor and Matanot Status

micro_segments.title: Reframing: both sides agree tovas hana'ah is not monetary; debate is about matanot ownership timing
Rabbi: matanot were already owned by ancestor kohen so all tevel is fully owned
Rabbi Yose bar Yehuda: matanot not yet present until separated, not pre-owned

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Resolution: Thief Penalty Overrides Ownership Analysis

micro_segments.title: Gemara rejects ownership debate; instead: rabbis penalized thief even though owner's stake is less
Logic: if owner only pays 88 for 100 stolen, thief escapes obligation to actual kohen/levi
Alternative: everyone agrees Shmuel (even one grain exempts pile) but debate about proper payment

macro_segments.title: Mei Chatas and Eifer Parah: Service for Drawing Water and Transporting Ash, Not the Act Itself

micro_segments.title: Challenge: taking payment for sprinkling or mixing voids the ritual; how can it enable marriage?
Abaye's distinction: forbidden to take payment for the mitzvah acts (sprinkling/mixing)
Permitted payment is for hava'ah u'milui (transporting ash, drawing water)—service, not mitzvah
Mishnah language confirms: mei chatas and eifer parah (water and ashes) not sprinkling/mixing

topical_tags.term: Mar Yehuda / Rav Yosef / Shmuel bar Rabba
Gemara on Theft and Owner Stake
